unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Bring together your strongest vocabulary, reading, grammar, speaking and writing from Units 1-9. Create an evidence-led inquiry product for a defined audience, attribute and evaluate sources, defend conclusions and reflect on your Grade 8 growth.
unit.learningPath: Curate and annotate evidence. Form an inquiry question. Compare and verify sources. Plan and draft cohesive sections. Revise from feedback. Present and defend. Reflect toward Grade 9. Complete the final course quiz.

visual.alt: A Grade 8 learner defending a researched English capstone

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Using few words to say something clearly.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher asked us to write a concise summary of the story.
practiceSentences: The teacher asked us to write a concise summary of the story.
For the capstone anthology, each pupil wrote a concise author biography of exactly fifty words.
How do you keep a conclusion concise without making it feel abrupt or unfinished?
"A concise argument respects your audience," Theo reminded the younger debaters he was mentoring.
Looking back over Grade Eight, my greatest improvement is writing concise paragraphs that still carry weight.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - n - c - i - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'concise', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Languages and Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A structure used to block people or vehicles from passing.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The protesters built a barricade across the road using old crates.
practiceSentences: The protesters built a barricade across the road using old crates.
Stage crews built a low barricade so the audience could not wander behind the scenery.
During the flood drill, who is responsible for setting the barricade across the lower corridor?
"Treat fear like a barricade," said the guest speaker, "something to dismantle plank by plank."
In my final reflection, I described self-doubt as a barricade that this year taught me to climb.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - a - r - r - i - c - a - d - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'barricade', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Languages and Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A strong feeling of enthusiasm and eagerness.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She studied with great zeal because she wanted the top grade.
practiceSentences: She studied with great zeal because she wanted the top grade.
Adam organised the end-of-year showcase with a zeal that inspired even the most reluctant helpers.
Where does your zeal for storytelling come from, and how will you keep it alive next year?
"Channel that zeal into your final project," the teacher advised, "and the marks will follow."
My zeal for reading grew this year, mostly because I finally found novels that mirrored my world.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: z - e - a - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'zeal', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Languages and Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The length of time that a person or thing lives or lasts.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Eating healthy food can improve your longevity.
practiceSentences: Eating healthy food can improve your longevity.
The time capsule project made everyone consider the longevity of the messages we leave behind.
What gives certain school traditions their longevity while others fade after a single year?
"Handwritten letters have a longevity that deleted messages never achieve," argued Samira in her speech.
I hope the friendships formed in this class have the longevity our teachers keep predicting.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - o - n - g - e - v - i - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'longevity', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Languages and Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To feel very happy and excited, especially because of a success or victory.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: After winning the game, the team began to exult and celebrate their victory.
practiceSentences: After winning the game, the team began to exult and celebrate their victory.
The whole year group seemed to exult when the final projects were displayed in the hall.
Is it possible to exult in a classmate's success without feeling a flicker of envy?
"Let us exult quietly," whispered Maya, "since the younger classes are still sitting their examinations."
I exult a little every time an essay returns with fewer corrections than the one before.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - x - u - l - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'exult', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Design, Packaging, and Sustainability.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To feel strong dislike or hatred for something or someone.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I loathe doing homework on weekends when the weather is beautiful.
practiceSentences: I loathe doing homework on weekends when the weather is beautiful.
Sami used to loathe editing his drafts, but the capstone project changed his attitude entirely.
Why do so many students loathe public speaking when conversation comes to them so easily?
"I loathe leaving a story unfinished," confessed Theo, "so I stayed up completing the final chapter."
I once loathed grammar drills, yet now I see how they quietly strengthened my writing.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - o - a - t - h - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'loathe', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Design, Packaging, and Sustainability.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To take control of something, especially a position of power, without the right to do so.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The student tried to usurp the class president's role by making all the decisions.
practiceSentences: The student tried to usurp the class president's role by making all the decisions.
In the class novel, a jealous adviser plots to usurp the young queen's rightful throne.
Can a loud committee member usurp a chairperson's role without anyone formally objecting?
"Don't let panic usurp your preparation," the examiner said; "trust the work you have done."
I let social media usurp my reading time last term, a mistake I refuse to repeat.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: u - s - u - r - p
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'usurp', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Design, Packaging, and Sustainability.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To pretend to have something or to be something that you are not.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She tried to feign happiness, but everyone could see she was sad.
practiceSentences: She tried to feign happiness, but everyone could see she was sad.
Actors in the showcase had to feign astonishment convincingly during the courtroom scene.
Why do people feign indifference about results they have secretly worked towards all year?
"Never feign understanding in class," urged Mr Hassan; "an honest question helps everyone."
I used to feign confidence before presentations, but genuine preparation turned the act into reality.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - e - i - g - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'feign', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Design, Packaging, and Sustainability.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To stop something from happening or developing; to hold back.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher tried to stifle the noise in the classroom by asking everyone to be quiet.
practiceSentences: The teacher tried to stifle the noise in the classroom by asking everyone to be quiet.
Heavy nerves can stifle a speaker's voice, which is why we rehearse in friendly groups first.
Does strict grading stifle creativity, or does it push writers to sharpen their craft?
"Don't stifle that idea," said Samira, "strange beginnings often become the best capstone pieces."
I had to stifle a laugh during the serious scene when the cardboard tree toppled over.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - t - i - f - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'stifle', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Community and Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To become smaller, less strong, or less important over time.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: As the evening went on, the excitement at the party began to wane.
practiceSentences: As the evening went on, the excitement at the party began to wane.
Enthusiasm for revision tends to wane in June, just when steady effort matters most.
Did your interest in the mystery novel wane once the culprit's identity became obvious?
"My energy may wane," joked the coach, "but my expectations for this team never will."
I watched my stage fright wane through the year as each presentation felt smaller than the last.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: w - a - n - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'wane', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Community and Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To remember and honour someone or something, often with a ceremony.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We commemorate our school's anniversary every year with a big celebration.
practiceSentences: We commemorate our school's anniversary every year with a big celebration.
The mural outside the library will commemorate ten years of the school's reading festival.
How should our year group commemorate the teachers who guided us through Grade Eight?
"We planted an olive tree to commemorate the school's founders," explained the head of year.
I chose to commemorate my grandmother in my capstone poem, weaving her sayings into every stanza.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - m - m - e - m - o - r - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'commemorate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Community and Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To express thoughts and ideas clearly and effectively.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She was able to articulate her opinion during the class discussion.
practiceSentences: She was able to articulate her opinion during the class discussion.
The capstone interviews taught even quiet pupils to articulate their goals with surprising confidence.
Can you articulate exactly why the closing paragraph of your essay feels unfinished?
"Take a breath and articulate the problem slowly," advised Maya during the peer feedback session.
I struggled to articulate my feelings about leaving Grade Eight until the farewell assembly began.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - r - t - i - c - u - l - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'articulate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Community and Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A natural ability to do something well.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She has a great aptitude for learning new languages quickly.
practiceSentences: She has a great aptitude for learning new languages quickly.
The capstone projects revealed that Theo has a genuine aptitude for scriptwriting and dialogue.
Does an aptitude for languages guarantee success, or does perseverance matter far more?
"Your aptitude for editing is rare," the teacher told Samira; "consider journalism seriously."
Discovering my aptitude for persuasive writing was the most unexpected gift of this school year.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - p - t - i - t - u - d - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'aptitude', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Advertising and Persuasion.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person or group that opposes or fights against another.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In the competition, each team tried to outsmart their adversary to win.
practiceSentences: In the competition, each team tried to outsmart their adversary to win.
In the final debate, each speaker treated their adversary with pointed but respectful courtesy.
Is procrastination the true adversary of every student during examination season?
"Shake hands with your adversary before and after the match," insisted the chess coach.
I have learnt to see a strong adversary as a teacher who exposes my weakest arguments.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - d - v - e - r - s - a - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'adversary', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Advertising and Persuasion.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Having the ability to achieve things in a clever and often deceptive way.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The cunning fox tricked the farmer and stole some chickens.
practiceSentences: The cunning fox tricked the farmer and stole some chickens.
The play's villain relied on cunning rather than strength, forging letters and spreading whispers.
Where is the line between clever strategy and cunning that shades into dishonesty?
"That was a cunning move," admitted Adam, watching his rook fall into the trap.
I admire the cunning of mystery writers who hide the vital clue in plain sight.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - u - n - n - i - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'cunning', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Advertising and Persuasion.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The car gained momentum as it rolled down the hill.
practiceSentences: The car gained momentum as it rolled down the hill.
The fundraising campaign gathered momentum once the local bakery offered to match every donation.
How do you keep your revision momentum going after the first difficult week?
"Don't lose momentum now," urged the coach; "the final stretch decides everything."
My reading momentum built all year, and I finished more novels this term than ever before.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - o - m - e - n - t - u - m
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'momentum', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Advertising and Persuasion.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: to hesitate or lose strength when doing something
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She began to falter in her speech when she saw the large crowd.
practiceSentences: She began to falter in her speech when she saw the large crowd.
Even confident speakers falter occasionally; the skill lies in recovering without losing the thread.
Did your resolve falter when the judges announced a surprise final round?
"If you falter mid-sentence, pause and smile," advised Samira; "the audience barely notices."
My handwriting began to falter near the end of the three-hour examination, but my ideas stayed firm.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - a - l - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'falter', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Natural Resources and Sustainability.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: clean, fresh, and in its original condition; not spoiled or damaged
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The lake was so pristine that we could see all the fish swimming in it.
practiceSentences: The lake was so pristine that we could see all the fish swimming in it.
Maya kept her capstone portfolio in pristine condition, each page protected by a plastic sleeve.
How has the old library kept its wooden shelves so pristine after eighty years of use?
"Hand in pristine copies," the teacher requested, "because smudged work distracts the moderators."
I love opening a pristine notebook in September, though its emptiness is slightly terrifying too.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - i - s - t - i - n - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'pristine', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Natural Resources and Sustainability.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: making someone believe something that is not true; misleading
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The magician's tricks were so deceptive that the audience could not figure them out.
practiceSentences: The magician's tricks were so deceptive that the audience could not figure them out.
The essay question looked deceptive in its simplicity, hiding three separate demands within one line.
Why are averages sometimes deceptive when we compare test results between two classes?
"Calm water can be deceptive," warned the lifeguard, "so respect the flags at all times."
I fell for the deceptive charm of an advert once, and the gadget broke within a week.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - c - e - p - t - i - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'deceptive', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Natural Resources and Sustainability.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: lacking experience or sophistication; easily fooled or deceived
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She was so naive that she believed every story she heard.
practiceSentences: She was so naive that she believed every story she heard.
Looking back, Sami admitted he had been naive to leave his project until the final weekend.
Is it naive to believe that every online review is written by a real customer?
"I was naive about deadlines in September," laughed Theo, "but Grade Eight cured me quickly."
Being hopeful is not the same as being naive; hope simply needs a plan behind it.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - a - i - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'naive', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Natural Resources and Sustainability.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A very cruel or terrible act
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The war led to many atrocities that shocked the world.
practiceSentences: The war led to many atrocities that shocked the world.
Our history readings examined how journalists risked everything to document each atrocity of the war.
How can literature help future generations understand an atrocity without overwhelming them with despair?
"Every atrocity begins with dehumanising language," the guest historian reminded our assembly.
Reading survivor testimony taught me that recording an atrocity honestly is itself an act of courage.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - t - r - o - c - i - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'atrocity', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Natural Wonders and Conservation.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A place where someone is going or something is being sent
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Our destination for the field trip is the national park.
practiceSentences: Our destination for the field trip is the national park.
The class voted on a destination for the leavers' trip, choosing the science museum by miles.
Is the destination really more important than the journey, as Adam claimed in his speech?
"Our final destination is the coast," announced the driver, "with one rest stop along the way."
I marked every destination from our class novel on a map, tracing the hero's remarkable route.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - s - t - i - n - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'destination', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Natural Wonders and Conservation.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To stop doing something
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher asked the students to desist from talking during the lesson.
practiceSentences: The teacher asked the students to desist from talking during the lesson.
The referee ordered both teams to desist from arguing and restart the match immediately.
Will the noisy construction crew desist before our afternoon listening examination begins?
"Kindly desist from tapping your pen," whispered Samira, "or I shall lose my train of thought."
I finally desisted from checking my phone while studying, and my focus improved within days.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - s - i - s - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'desist', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Natural Wonders and Conservation.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make someone decide not to do something
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The warning signs deter hikers from taking the dangerous path.
practiceSentences: The warning signs deter hikers from taking the dangerous path.
New locks on the bicycle shed should deter thieves far better than the faded warning sign.
Would a heavier penalty actually deter latecomers, or simply make them more inventive with excuses?
"Let no setback deter you," wrote the headteacher in our yearbooks, "least of all a single grade."
Rain could not deter our class from finishing the charity walk we had promised to complete.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'deter', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Natural Wonders and Conservation.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Kind and gentle; not harmful or dangerous.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher's benign smile made the students feel comfortable in class.
practiceSentences: The teacher's benign smile made the students feel comfortable in class.
The new librarian's benign manner soon persuaded even the shyest pupils to ask for help.
Was the strange email actually benign, or should Theo report it to the ICT office?
"The lump proved benign," the vet assured Maya, "so your cat needs only rest."
I misjudged the deputy head completely; behind the stern voice is a genuinely benign teacher.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - e - n - i - g - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'benign', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Historical Figures and Legacy.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A temporary break from something difficult or unpleasant.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: After studying hard for exams, I took a reprieve by playing video games for an hour.
practiceSentences: After studying hard for exams, I took a reprieve by playing video games for an hour.
The postponed test gave the exhausted class a welcome reprieve of two whole days.
Is a short reprieve from screens each evening enough to improve your concentration?
"Consider this a reprieve, not a cancellation," smiled the teacher, rescheduling the spelling quiz.
Friday's rain offered a reprieve from sports practice, which I secretly spent finishing my novel.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - p - r - i - e - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'reprieve', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Historical Figures and Legacy.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A problem or fault in something that makes it not work properly.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The toy had a defect, so it did not work when we tried to use it.
practiceSentences: The toy had a defect, so it did not work when we tried to use it.
A printing defect left twenty copies of the yearbook without their final, most important page.
Did the engineers find the defect before or after the bridge was opened to traffic?
"This torch has a defect," reported Sami, "it flickers whenever you tilt it sideways."
I returned the headphones because of a small defect, and the shop replaced them without fuss.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - f - e - c - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'defect', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Historical Figures and Legacy.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person's usual mood or way of thinking.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Her cheerful disposition makes everyone around her feel happy.
practiceSentences: Her cheerful disposition makes everyone around her feel happy.
Samira's calm disposition made her the obvious choice to chair the heated planning meeting.
How much of a person's disposition is inherited, and how much is shaped by habit?
"A cheerful disposition is contagious," observed the coach, "so choose your companions thoughtfully."
My disposition improves remarkably after a morning walk, a pattern I only noticed this year.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - s - p - o - s - i - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'disposition', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Historical Figures and Legacy.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A planned series of actions carried out over time to achieve an important goal.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The students launched a campaign to raise money for their school library.
practiceSentences: Maya's recycling campaign began with one poster and ended with bins in every classroom.
What turned a single letter to the council into a whole campaign for safer crossings?
"Join our campaign against food waste," urged the canteen banner, "and take only what you need."
I admire my grandfather's lifelong campaign for literacy, teaching neighbours to read every Saturday morning.
The election campaign lasted six weeks and ended with a debate in the town hall.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - a - m - p - a - i - g - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the learner to recall a campaign they have seen at school or in their town, describe what it was trying to change, and use the word 'campaign' in a full sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make something clear or easier to understand.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Can you clarify what you mean by that?
practiceSentences: Can you clarify what you mean by that?
The examiner paused to clarify the instructions after several puzzled hands rose at once.
Could you clarify whether the reflection should describe one project or the entire year?
"Let me clarify my earlier point," said Adam, "because I muddled the two poets' names."
Writing summaries helps me clarify my own thinking far better than simply rereading the chapter.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - l - a - r - i - f - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'clarify', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Global Storytelling and Creativity.

outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Capstone portfolio, inquiry product, source record, oral defence and reflection

readings.title: Review text 1: Languages and Communication: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 1 of your Year 8 English course at Ehel Academy. In this unit you will explore the powerful theme of languages and communication -- how people share ideas, how rumours spread, and how the truth can be lost or found depending on how carefully we listen and speak. You will read a compelling story set in Mogadishu, learn important grammar structures, build a rich vocabulary, and practise all four language skills: reading, listening, speaking, and writing.
This unit is designed for self-paced learning. You will work through the material at your own speed, with the support of your AI tutor available around the clock and your teacher sessions twice a week. Every section includes activities, tips, and prompts to help you deepen your understanding. By the end of this unit you will be a more confident, thoughtful communicator -- someone who questions what they hear, chooses words with care, and speaks with purpose.
The story at the heart of this unit, "Whispers Beyond the Gate," follows a group of students in Mogadishu who discover how quickly misinformation can spread through a community and what happens when people act on rumour rather than fact. As you study this story, you will encounter the vocabulary words from your word list used in real contexts, and you will see the grammar patterns you are learning woven naturally into the narrative.
Throughout this lesson hub you will find "Ask Your AI Tutor" prompts. These are suggestions for questions you can ask your AI tutor at any time. Think of the tutor as a patient, knowledgeable study partner who is always ready to explain, give examples, or quiz you. Do not be shy about using it -- the more you interact, the faster you will learn.
By the end of this unit, you should be able to:
Use the contrast conjunctions although, while, and whereas accurately in spoken and written English.
Recognise and use -ing forms (gerunds) as subjects and objects in sentences.
Form and use the present continuous tense to describe actions happening now or in progress.
Identify and use abstract nouns such as serenity, foresight, dissent, and zeal in appropriate contexts.
Convert direct speech into reported speech, adjusting tense, pronouns, and time expressions correctly.
Construct zero and first conditional sentences to express facts and future possibilities.
Use connectives (because, so that, although, however, therefore) to link ideas logically.
Understand and use multi-word verbs (phrasal verbs) such as give in, carry out, and speak up.
Read a full-length story critically, identifying themes, character development, and moral lessons.
Expand your vocabulary by learning and practising 33 new words from the Unit 1 word list.
Write a structured paragraph or short essay that uses contrast conjunctions and abstract nouns.
Speak about social issues with clarity, supporting your opinions with evidence.
Apply critical thinking to evaluate information and distinguish fact from rumour.
Before you dive into the reading and grammar, take some time to preview the key vocabulary for this unit. These are words you will encounter repeatedly in the story, grammar exercises, and writing tasks. Familiarising yourself with them now will make everything that follows easier to understand.
Your Unit 1 vocabulary list contains 33 words organised into groups by part of speech. Here is a quick overview of the groups and some highlighted words from each:
Nouns: barricade, zeal, definition, foresight, defiance, dissent, serenity, revelation, longevity, squalor, embargo, quibble
These nouns include several abstract nouns -- words for ideas, feelings, and qualities you cannot physically touch. Words like zeal (strong enthusiasm), serenity (calmness and peace), and foresight (the ability to plan for the future) are especially important in this unit because they appear in both the story and the grammar lessons on abstract nouns.
Verbs: allot, accustom, assimilate, concede, dispel, cite
These verbs describe mental and social actions. For example, to assimilate means to absorb and understand new information, and to cite means to refer to a source of evidence. You will use these verbs in your writing and speaking tasks.

readings.title: Review text 2: Design, Packaging, and Sustainability: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
restitution — the act of giving back something that was lost or taken away
hindrance — something that makes it difficult for you to do something
tendril — a thin, curly part of a plant that helps it climb or hold onto something
The verbs in this unit describe strong actions and emotions. Many of them express feelings, strategies, and consequences.
exult — to feel very happy and excited, especially because of a success or victory
loathe — to feel strong dislike or hatred for something or someone
usurp — to take control of something without the right to do so
feign — to pretend to have something or to be something that you are not
intervene — to come between people or groups to help settle a problem
obstruct — to block something so that it cannot move or be seen
avert — to prevent something bad from happening
inundate — to flood or overwhelm with a large amount of something
flourish — to grow or develop in a healthy or strong way
decipher — to figure out or understand something difficult to read or understand
rebuke — to speak to someone in a way that shows you are angry because they did something wrong
quell — to stop something, especially a feeling or a situation, from becoming worse
The adjectives in this unit describe people, situations, and qualities. They will help you write more vividly and speak more precisely.
adept — very skilled or good at something
impassive — not showing any emotion or feeling; calm and controlled
sociable — someone who likes to talk and spend time with other people
implicit — suggested or understood without being directly stated
exhilarating — very exciting and happy
pertinent — related to the topic or important for understanding something
erratic — not regular or consistent; unpredictable
luminous — giving off light; bright or shining
oblique — not straight or direct; slanted or angled
monotonous — boring because it is always the same and never changes
Write each new word on a flashcard with the definition on one side and an example sentence on the other.
Try to use at least three new vocabulary words in your conversations each day.
Group the words by part of speech (nouns, verbs, adjectives) to help you remember them.
This unit covers several important grammar topics that will help you express yourself more clearly and precisely. Your primary grammar focus is on partitive expressions, but you will also practise zero and first conditionals, relative clauses, comparatives and superlatives, passive voice, and questions beginning with prepositions. Let us begin with an overview of each topic.
Partitive Expressions — Your Primary Focus
Partitive expressions help you talk about a portion or part of something. Instead of saying "I want cake," you can say "I want a piece of cake." Instead of saying "She felt happiness," you can say "She felt a surge of happiness." These expressions make your language more specific and more natural.
Here is how partitive expressions work:
Choose the right partitive word. Common ones include: a piece of, a cup of, a slice of, a bit of, a lot of, a surge of, a multitude of, a handful of, a drop of.
Combine them. Put the partitive word before the noun: a piece of cake, a glass of water, a surge of indignation, a multitude of calls.
Use them in sentences. For example: "There was a surge of excitement when Samira went on air." Or: "A multitude of listeners called the station."
Why do partitive expressions matter for this unit? In the story "Signal in the Static," Samira and Ibrahim deal with large quantities of information, strong emotions, and complex situations. Partitive expressions help the author describe these things precisely. When you read phrases like "a surge of indignation" or "a multitude of calls," you are seeing partitive expressions in action. Learning to use them will make your own writing more vivid and more mature.

readings.title: Review text 3: Community and Environment: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
🧭 How to use this unit
This is your main lesson file. It has all the content you need to study for Unit 3, organised into clear sections.
You also have three companion files: the Story ("Rising Tide"), the Grammar Reference, and the Vocabulary Word Book.
Study a little every day. Read actively, write in your notebook, and speak English out loud. Use your AI tutor whenever you are stuck, and bring your questions to your teacher sessions twice a week.
Welcome to Unit 3: Community and Environment. In this unit, you will explore how young people can make a difference in their communities by protecting the environment. You will read a story called "Rising Tide" about a girl named Sophia who leads a youth movement to clean up a polluted bay in a coastal Kenyan city. Through this story and the activities in this lesson, you will build your vocabulary, sharpen your grammar skills, and develop your ability to speak and write persuasively about issues that matter to you.
The theme of this unit -- community and environment -- is directly relevant to your life. Whether you live near the coast, in a city, or in a rural area, environmental issues affect everyone. Learning to express your ideas about these issues clearly and persuasively is a valuable skill that will serve you well beyond this English course.
You will learn how to use clauses that end with prepositions to sound more natural in English, how to use the second conditional to talk about imaginary situations and possibilities, how to use the present continuous passive to describe actions in progress, and how to combine verbs and adjectives with the correct prepositions. These grammar skills will make your English more fluent and sophisticated.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand a complex narrative about community activism and environmental protection.
Use 33 new vocabulary words accurately in speaking and writing.
Construct clauses that end with prepositions naturally and correctly.
Form and use second conditional sentences to express imaginary situations and wishes.
Identify and create present continuous passive sentences to describe ongoing actions.
Combine verbs and adjectives with their correct prepositions, including using the -ing form after prepositions.
Write a persuasive speech or formal letter about an environmental issue in your community.
Speak confidently about community problems and propose solutions using the grammar structures from this unit.
Understand different perspectives on environmental issues and express your own viewpoint.
Use formal language structures such as "It is imperative that..." in appropriate contexts.
Before you begin reading and working through the activities, take a look at the key vocabulary for this unit. You will encounter these words throughout the story, grammar exercises, and activities. Open your Vocabulary companion file for full definitions, examples, and practice exercises. Here is a quick preview of the words grouped by type.
stifle -- to stop something from happening or developing | wane -- to become less over time | commemorate -- to remember and honour | articulate -- to express clearly | hasten -- to make faster | advocate -- to publicly support (also a noun) | affix -- to attach | facilitate -- to make easier | illuminate -- to light up or make clear | brandish -- to wave or hold excitedly
potential -- ability to develop | exasperation -- strong frustration | misgiving -- doubt or worry | perspective -- a way of thinking | debris -- broken pieces or waste | banter -- playful conversation
gaunt -- very thin and weak | coherent -- clear and logical | omniscient -- all-knowing | dire -- very serious | sporadic -- irregular, not steady | amiable -- friendly | dejected -- sad and disappointed | telltale -- revealing | emaciated -- extremely thin from lack of food | tedious -- boring and long | succinct -- brief and clear | rational -- logical, sensible | durable -- long-lasting | exuberant -- full of energy and joy | imperative -- extremely important (also a noun)

readings.title: Review text 4: Advertising and Persuasion: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
🧭 How to use this unit
This is your main lesson file. It has twelve sections to work through at your own pace.
You also have three companion files: Vocabulary, Grammar (Prepositions Preceding Nouns, Passive Voice, Participle Clauses, Reflexive Pronouns, Second Conditional, Comparatives, Compound Adjectives, and Reported Speech), and the Story "Code Name: Bonanza".
Study a little every day. Use your AI tutor whenever you are stuck, and bring your work to your teacher sessions twice a week.
In this unit, you will explore the language of advertising and persuasion. You will study how advertisers choose their words carefully, how they appeal to emotions, and how they use visual design to make products seem irresistible. You will also examine how persuasive language appears in everyday life — in speeches, debates, product reviews, and even conversations with friends. Understanding these techniques will make you a more attentive reader, a more shrewd consumer, and a more confident communicator.
The grammar focus of this unit is prepositions preceding nouns — small but powerful words like on, in, for, at, and with that appear before nouns to show relationships. You will notice them everywhere in advertising language: items on sale, products in stock, offers valid for a limited time. Alongside prepositions, you will study passive voice, participle clauses, reflexive pronouns, the second conditional, comparatives with "the more / the less", compound adjectives, and reported speech. These grammar tools will help you write more persuasive texts, analyse advertisements critically, and express your own opinions with precision.
You will also read the story "Code Name: Bonanza", set in an East African tech school where student teams compete in an innovation challenge. The story explores how persuasion, branding, and integrity interact in a competitive environment. Through the story, you will see how vocabulary words like cunning, adversary, momentum, and bonanza come alive in context, and you will discover that the most lasting success comes not from clever tricks but from honest effort and ethical strategy.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand passages about advertising techniques, persuasion, and ethical marketing, and discuss their ideas confidently.
Identify and use prepositions preceding nouns correctly in phrases like on sale, in stock, for a limited time, at a discount, and with interest.
Form and use passive voice sentences in the present and past: "The product was launched by the company" and "The advert is designed to attract young people."
Begin sentences with past participle clauses: "Created by a cunning team, the ad drew attention from thousands."
Use reflexive pronouns correctly: myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves — "She taught herself to code."
Form second conditional sentences to imagine unreal situations: "If I had more time, I would design a better poster."
Use comparatives with "the more / the less": "The more belligerent the tone, the less effective the message."
Form and use compound adjectives with hyphens: well-branded, fast-selling, multi-use, future-ready.
Change direct speech into reported speech: "She said that the offer was a concession."
Write a persuasive advertisement, a product review, or an analytical essay about advertising techniques.
Present your opinions about advertising clearly and persuasively in spoken English.
Use at least fifteen new vocabulary words from this unit confidently in speech and writing.
Think critically about how advertising shapes consumer behaviour, the ethics of persuasion, and the difference between information and manipulation.
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the grammar lessons in the Grammar file (Prepositions Preceding Nouns, Passive Voice, Participle Clauses, Reflexive Pronouns, Second Conditional, Comparatives, Compound Adjectives, Reported Speech).
Read the Story file, "Code Name: Bonanza," and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all twelve sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Before you read the passage below, make sure you know these important words. They appear throughout the unit. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary file or ask your AI tutor to explain it.

readings.title: Review text 5: Natural Resources and Sustainability: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Here are the vocabulary words for this unit, grouped into categories to help you see connections between them. You do not need to memorise all of them right now—just get a sense of the words and their general meanings. You will study each one in detail later.
Feelings and Attitudes: somber, adamant, haughty, disdain, naive, persistent
Describing Quality and Condition: pristine, deceptive, dismal, egregious, threadbare, terse, copious
Actions and Processes: falter, modify, surpass, avail, subside, pivot, discern, instill
Ideas and Language: principle, dialect, antonym, tactic, transition, attribute, credible
Problems and Mistakes: amiss, awry, precise
Look at the words listed above. For each category, choose two words that you have never seen before. Write them down in your notebook. Then try to guess their meaning based on the category name. For example, if you chose egregious from the "Describing Quality and Condition" group, you might guess it describes something in a strong way. After guessing, check the vocabulary document to see how close you were.
This preview activity activates your brain and prepares it to recognise the words when they appear in the story and grammar exercises. Research shows that even brief exposure to a new word before reading helps you learn it faster and remember it longer.
Some of these words may already be familiar to you. Words like precise, naive, and transition appear in everyday English. If you already know a word, that is great—focus your energy on the words that are completely new. However, even familiar words can have surprising uses. For example, avail is an unusual word that most English speakers rarely use in casual conversation, but it appears in formal writing and in set phrases like "to no avail." Pay attention to how each word is used in the story, because context often reveals shades of meaning that a dictionary definition cannot capture.
This unit covers several important grammar topics. Your main focus is on complex noun phrases, but you will also work with the future passive voice, connectives for contrast and purpose, and past modals. In this section, you will get an overview of each topic. For detailed explanations, examples, exercises, and answer keys, refer to the Grammar Reference document.
A complex noun phrase is a noun with extra words that give more information about it. Instead of writing "the mistake," you might write "the egregious environmental mistake that went unnoticed for years." The extra words—called modifiers—make your writing more detailed and precise.
There are two types of modifiers. Pre-modifiers come before the noun and are usually adjectives or determiners: "the pristine blue water." Post-modifiers come after the noun and can be prepositional phrases, relative clauses, or participle phrases: "the water in the lake," "the boy who helped gather samples."
Mastering complex noun phrases is one of the most important steps in improving your academic English. When you can describe things in detail using a single noun phrase, your writing becomes more efficient and more sophisticated. You will practise identifying complex noun phrases in the story and building your own in the exercises.
Why does this matter for you? In academic writing, in exam answers, and in formal speaking, you need to communicate a lot of information clearly and concisely. Complex noun phrases let you pack meaning into fewer words. Instead of writing three short sentences—"There was a mistake. It was environmental. It was egregious."—you can write one powerful sentence: "The egregious environmental mistake caused widespread damage."
The future passive follows the pattern: Subject + will be + past participle. You use it when the important thing is what will happen, not who will do it. For example: "The purification systems will be installed by December." This structure appears in formal writing, news reports, and official announcements. In the story, the mayor uses the future passive when announcing the town’s plans.
You already know the present and past passive from earlier units. The future passive simply adds "will" to the pattern. The key challenge is remembering to include "be" and to use the correct past participle form of the verb. Common errors include writing "will installed" instead of "will be installed" or using the base form instead of the past participle.

grammar.title: Sentence accuracy audit
grammar.explanation: Review agreement, tense and complete sentences.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Every sentence needs a clear subject and verb.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Correct six sentences from your portfolio.

grammar.title: Tense control
grammar.explanation: Check that past, present and future time are clear.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Yesterday I visited. Today I learn. Tomorrow I will present.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Revise six verbs and explain each tense choice.

grammar.title: Questions and responses
grammar.explanation: Use accurate Wh- and yes/no questions.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Where did you learn this? Why is it important?
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Write and answer six audience questions.

grammar.title: Description and precision
grammar.explanation: Improve nouns, verbs, adjectives and adverbs.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: The thoughtful learner explained the idea clearly.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Joining ideas
grammar.explanation: Connect ideas with and, but, because, so and when.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: I revised my story because feedback helped me.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Final editing conference
grammar.explanation: Apply capitals, spelling, punctuation and paragraphing.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read, mark, revise, then read again.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

writing.title: Portfolio evidence annotations
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a precise paragraph about each of four selected pieces and explain what each demonstrates.
writing.modelText: This revised report shows that I can organize evidence, evaluate a viewpoint and explain the effect of my choices.
writing.expectedLength: Five to seven sustained paragraphs with source evidence
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized and attributed relevant evidence; evaluated source reliability and viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use an inquiry organizer, source record, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate competing source choices, address a counterargument, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.title: Inquiry proposal
writing.promptAndInstructions: Explain your question, audience, approved sources, product and plan in four organized paragraphs.
writing.modelText: My inquiry will investigate how communities can use digital tools responsibly by comparing two approved sources.

writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized and attributed relevant evidence; evaluated source reliability and viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use an inquiry organizer, source record, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate competing source choices, address a counterargument, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.title: Evidence-led first draft

writing.modelText: Use a focused introduction, cohesive sections, synthesized evidence, a counterargument and a conclusion suited to your audience.
writing.expectedLength: Five to seven sustained paragraphs with source evidence
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized and attributed relevant evidence; evaluated source reliability and viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use an inquiry organizer, source record, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate competing source choices, address a counterargument, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.title: Substantive revision from feedback
writing.promptAndInstructions: Record four feedback points and rewrite the sections they improve.

writing.sentenceStarter: One substantial improvement I made was...
writing.expectedLength: Five to seven sustained paragraphs with source evidence
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized and attributed relevant evidence; evaluated source reliability and viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use an inquiry organizer, source record, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate competing source choices, address a counterargument, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized and attributed relevant evidence; evaluated source reliability and viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use an inquiry organizer, source record, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate competing source choices, address a counterargument, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.support: Use an inquiry organizer, source record, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate competing source choices, address a counterargument, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

quizzes.question: What does 'concise' mean?
quizzes.options: A statement that explains the meaning of a word. | A strong feeling of enthusiasm and eagerness. | The length of time that a person or thing lives or lasts. | Using few words to say something clearly.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Using few words to say something clearly.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher asked us to write a concise summary of the story.

quizzes.question: What does 'barricade' mean?
quizzes.options: A statement that explains the meaning of a word. | A structure used to block people or vehicles from passing. | The length of time that a person or thing lives or lasts. | To give or set aside a certain amount for a specific purpose.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A structure used to block people or vehicles from passing.
quizzes.explanation: The protesters built a barricade across the road using old crates.

quizzes.question: What does 'exult' mean?
quizzes.options: To come between two people or groups to help settle a problem or stop something bad. | To feel very happy and excited, especially because of a success or victory. | To pretend to have something or to be something that you are not. | To take control of something, especially a position of power, without the right to do so.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To feel very happy and excited, especially because of a success or victory.
quizzes.explanation: After winning the game, the team began to exult and celebrate their victory.

quizzes.question: What does 'loathe' mean?
quizzes.options: To block something so that it cannot move or be seen. | To come between two people or groups to help settle a problem or stop something bad. | To feel strong dislike or hatred for something or someone. | To pretend to have something or to be something that you are not.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To feel strong dislike or hatred for something or someone.
quizzes.explanation: I loathe doing homework on weekends when the weather is beautiful.

quizzes.question: What does 'stifle' mean?
quizzes.options: To express thoughts and ideas clearly and effectively. | To make something happen faster; to hurry. | To remember and honour someone or something, often with a ceremony. | To stop something from happening or developing; to hold back.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To stop something from happening or developing; to hold back.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher tried to stifle the noise in the classroom by asking everyone to be quiet.

quizzes.question: What does 'wane' mean?
quizzes.options: To become smaller, less strong, or less important over time. | To express thoughts and ideas clearly and effectively. | To make something happen faster; to hurry. | Verb: to publicly support or recommend. Noun: a person who supports a cause.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To become smaller, less strong, or less important over time.
quizzes.explanation: As the evening went on, the excitement at the party began to wane.

quizzes.question: What does 'aptitude' mean?
quizzes.options: A natural ability to do something well. | A situation or event that produces a large amount of something good. | Having the ability to achieve things in a clever and often deceptive way. | The force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A natural ability to do something well.
quizzes.explanation: She has a great aptitude for learning new languages quickly.

quizzes.question: What does 'adversary' mean?
quizzes.options: A person or group that opposes or fights against another. | A situation or event that produces a large amount of something good. | Something that is given up or allowed in order to reach an agreement. | The force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person or group that opposes or fights against another.
quizzes.explanation: In the competition, each team tried to outsmart their adversary to win.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Review text 1: Languages and Communication: source text 1': Identify and use abstract nouns such as serenity, foresight, dissent, and _____ in appropriate contexts.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Identify and use abstract nouns such as serenity, foresight, dissent, and zeal in appropriate contexts.

Using few words to say something clearly. The teacher asked us to write a concise summary of the story.
A structure used to block people or vehicles from passing. The protesters built a barricade across the road using old crates.
To feel very happy and excited, especially because of a success or victory. After winning the game, the team began to exult and celebrate their victory.
To feel strong dislike or hatred for something or someone. I loathe doing homework on weekends when the weather is beautiful.
To stop something from happening or developing; to hold back. The teacher tried to stifle the noise in the classroom by asking everyone to be quiet.
To become smaller, less strong, or less important over time. As the evening went on, the excitement at the party began to wane.
A natural ability to do something well. She has a great aptitude for learning new languages quickly.
A person or group that opposes or fights against another. In the competition, each team tried to outsmart their adversary to win.
to hesitate or lose strength when doing something She began to falter in her speech when she saw the large crowd.
clean, fresh, and in its original condition; not spoiled or damaged The lake was so pristine that we could see all the fish swimming in it.
